_**Agent (auto-low)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Licensing is the key concern for storing NASB1995 internally.

NASB1995 is copyright by the Lockman Foundation. Up to 1,000 verses may be quoted without permission for personal use. Bulk storage or scraping is prohibited without explicit license. Full Bible storage in a public repository is not allowed.

A private subset under 1,000 verses may be acceptable for personal use. API.Bible offers on-demand access without local storage. A gitignored local file is another option. I am checking API.Bible for NASB1995 availability.

Three concrete options are being weighed.

A subset of NASB1995 verses could live in `data/bible/nasb1995/`. Only verses already quoted or frequently used would be included. This keeps the total under the 1,000 verse limit.

A gitignored local file is another path. The user would download a licensed copy from Logos or Olive Tree. A script would look up verses on demand.

API.Bible or a Scripture API could fetch verses at write time. This avoids local storage entirely. An API key would go in `.dev.vars`.
</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

[API.Bible](https://api.bible/api-key-guide)\n5. [Bibles | API.Bible Documentation](https://docs.api.bible/guides/bibles/)\n\nSynthesis:\nAPI.Bible provides programmatic access to various Bible translations, including the New American Standard Bible (NASB), for use in software applications [1][2]. To retrieve scripture, you must first authenticate with an API key and use the /bibles endpoint to locate the correct bible_id for the specific version you need [3][4]. For the NASB edition available on their platform, the bible_id is a761ca71e0b3ddcf-01 [5]. You can then fetch verses using the endpoint structure /v1/bibles/{bible_id}/verses/{reference}, replacing the placeholders with your specific IDs and desired chapter or verse [3].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eAPI.Bible Demo\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://demo.api.bible/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eAPI.Bible Demo\n\n### API.Bible NextJS Demo\n\n##### What is API.Bible?\n\n`https://api.bible`\n\nAPI.Bible is a tool for developers that provides access to a massive catalog of Bibles, including popular translations like NIV, NKJV, NASB, The Message, CST, NLT, The Amplified Bible, GNT, and more, through a fully-fledged API.\n\nHistorically, licensing Bibles has been a difficult and time-consuming process that had to be repeated for every translation required. API.Bible solves this hurdle for digital Bible use. We have collaborated with dozens of IP holders and gathered a massive catalogue of open access Bibles to provide you with everything you need to get the Bible into your app, all under a single license.\n\n### WELCOME.\n\nWelcome to the API.Bible NextJS Demo application! <think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Checking whether NASB1995 can be stored internally for verse quoting.

Lockman Foundation licensing restricts NASB1995 to 1000 verses in electronic systems without permission. The full dataset cannot be publicly hosted. Existing notes cite references but do not support verse lookup. A practical option is a gitignored local cache file for accurate quoting.

A script can query API.Bible and cache results locally. The API key stays in the gitignored `.dev.vars` file. Agents can reference this in a skill or README. Extracting only the note citations yields a subset under the 1000 verse limit. That subset could be committed with a copyright notice. Formal Lockman permission would allow storing the full text in a gitignored file. Implementation will wait for the user to choose an approach.

I will ask whether to build the full verse lookup system or just the minimal cache script.
</details></think>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com &amp;&amp; python3 - &lt;&lt;&#39;PY&#39;
import re
from pathlib import Path

BOOKS = (
    r&#34;Matthew|Mark|Luke|John|Romans|Galatians|Ephesians|Philippians|Colossians|&#34;
    r&#34;1\s*(?:Cor|Tim|Pet|John|Sam|Kings|Chron)|2\s*(?:Cor|Tim|Pet|Sam|Kings|Chron)|&#34;
    r&#34;3\s*John|Hebrews|James|Jude|Revelation|Genesis|Exodus|Leviticus|Deuteronomy|&#34;
    r&#34;Psalm|Proverbs|Isaiah|Jeremiah|Acts|Timothy|Titus|Philemon|Peter|Deuteronomy&#34;
)
REF = rf&#34;(?:{BOOKS})\s+(\d+):(\d+)(?:-(\d+))?&#34;
pat = re.compile(REF, re.I)

notes = Path(&#34;content/english/notes&#34;)
refs = set()
verse_count = 0
for p in notes.glob(&#34;*.md&#34;):
    text = p.read_text(encoding=&#34;utf-8&#34;)
    for m in pat.finditer(text):
        book = re.sub(r&#34;\s+&#34;, &#34; &#34;, m.group(0).split()[0]).title()
        if book.lower().startswith(&#34;1 &#34;) or book.lower().startswith(&#34;2 &#34;) or book.lower().startswith(&#34;3 &#34;):
            book = book  # keep
        refs.add(m.group(0).strip())
        start, end = int(m.group(2)), int(m.group(3) or m.group(2))
        verse_count += end - start + 1

print(f&#34;Unique ref strings: {len(refs)}&#34;)
print(f&#34;Approx verse span count: {verse_count}&#34;)
print(&#34;Sample:&#34;, sorted(refs)[:15])
PY</summary>
```bash
cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 - <<'PY'
import re
from pathlib import Path

BOOKS = (
    r"Matthew|Mark|Luke|John|Romans|Galatians|Ephesians|Philippians|Colossians|"
    r"1\s*(?:Cor|Tim|Pet|John|Sam|Kings|Chron)|2\s*(?:Cor|Tim|Pet|Sam|Kings|Chron)|"
    r"3\s*John|Hebrews|James|Jude|Revelation|Genesis|Exodus|Leviticus|Deuteronomy|"
    r"Psalm|Proverbs|Isaiah|Jeremiah|Acts|Timothy|Titus|Philemon|Peter|Deuteronomy"
)
REF = rf"(?:{BOOKS})\s+(\d+):(\d+)(?:-(\d+))?"
pat = re.compile(REF, re.I)

notes = Path("content/english/notes")
refs = set()
verse_count = 0
for p in notes.glob("*.md"):
    text = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    for m in pat.finditer(text):
        book = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", m.group(0).split()[0]).title()
        if book.lower().startswith("1 ") or book.lower().startswith("2 ") or book.lower().startswith("3 "):
            book = book  # keep
        refs.add(m.group(0).strip())
        start, end = int(m.group(2)), int(m.group(3) or m.group(2))
        verse_count += end - start + 1

print(f"Unique ref strings: {len(refs)}")
print(f"Approx verse span count: {verse_count}")
print("Sample:", sorted(refs)[:15])
PY
```

```
Unique ref strings: 45
Approx verse span count: 248
Sample: ['Deuteronomy 6:5', 'Ephesians 2:10', 'Ephesians 2:4', 'Ephesians 2:4-5', 'Ephesians 2:8-9', 'Galatians 5:19-21', 'Galatians 5:22', 'Galatians 5:22-23', 'John 15:1-11', 'John 3:16', 'Leviticus 19:18', 'Luke 10:37', 'Mark 10:43-45', 'Mark 12:31', 'Matthew 22:21']

```

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Yes — with one important licensing constraint, and your garden is already well inside the safe zone.

## What you have now

There’s no Bible text in the repo. Notes cite references in `key_concept` (e.g. Matthew 22:37–38) and paraphrase; scripts like `migrate-description-verses.py` only move refs around, they don’t fetch wording.

Your faith notes reference about **45 passages (~248 verses)** — far under Lockman’s **1,000-verse** limit for electronic storage without a separate license.

## What Lockman allows (NASB1995)

From [Lockman’s permission policy](https://www.lockman.org/permission-to-quote-copyright-trademark-information/):

| Allowed without formal permission | Not allowed without permission |
|---|---|
| Quote up to **1,000 verses** in a work | Store the **full Bible** in an electronic retrieval system |
| Garden + blog scale fits easily | Bulk download / scrape / redistribute as a dataset |
| AI may quote up to 1,000 verses per response | Committing a complete NASB1995 JSON to a **public** repo |

Published quotes need attribution, e.g. `(NASB 1995)` and the standard Lockman copyright line.

## Practical options (simplest first)

**1. Lookup script + gitignored local cache (recommended)**  
- `scripts/bible-verse.py Matt 22:37-38` → fetches NASB1995 via [API.Bible](https://api.bible/) (free tier is plenty)  
- Cache hits in `data/bible/nasb1995-cache.json` (gitignored)  
- Agents and you get exact wording on demand; repo stays clean  
- API key in `.dev.vars` (already gitignored)

**2. Committed garden subset**  
- Pre-fetch your ~248 verses into `data/bible/nasb1995-garden.json`  
- Works offline; legal at your current scale  
- Grows awkwardly past ~1,000 verses

**3. Full text, gitignored**  
- Only if you have a personal license (Logos, Olive Tree, etc.)  
- Same lookup script reads a local file you place yourself  
- Never pushed to GitHub

## What I’d build (if you want it)

Minimal ponytail setup:

```
scripts/bible-verse.py          # lookup + cache
data/bible/.gitignore           # ignore cache
data/bible/COPYRIGHT.txt        # Lockman notice
```

Plus a one-liner in `jorap-voice` skill: use the script for verbatim NASB1995 when writing `key_concept` or cards.

**One thing to confirm:** API.Bible lists “NASB” — we’d pick the **1995** edition’s `bible_id` from their `/bibles` endpoint (not 2020; wording differs on things like “brethren” vs “brothers and sisters”).

---

Do you want me to scaffold option **1** (API + cache)? If you already have an API.Bible key, drop it in `.dev.vars` as `API_BIBLE_KEY=...` and I can wire it up and pre-warm the cache for your existing garden refs. If you’d rather stay fully offline, option **2** or **3** works too — say which you prefer.
